item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it was taken for the article by author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I keep coming back to this skip. It's short, effective, and thought-provoking, and not in the way a lot of modern skips are. Sometimes, I feel like modern skips are a bit one-note. This is not one note. This introduces an interesting concept, and expands on it in an interesting way. This isn't "short" in how it cuts out potentially interesting plot elements to create a thin, cut-to-the-chase story straight out of Fahrenheit 451. This is short in how it takes all of the elements a skip should have and compresses it down in a sensible way.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this skip. And my new official headcannon is that Dr Bright created SCP-3664 from this pizza box. Lol

Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Entirely agreed. Even in a mundane environment people need to let off a bit of steam once in a while. Now imagine you spend all your waking days in an organization of crazy secrecy dealing with mind-bending things day in and day out…try to survive THAT without a little sense of humor.

I get that the portrayal of the Foundation as a bunch of irresponsible goofballs (as was occasionally present in the past) isn't desirable, but it seems like it's taken too far at times to the point where anything that's even slightly humorous gets backlash. They're still people, and a little levity is good for them (and good for the reader, too - there need to be breaks from the horror stuff, or it's just desensitizing).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly I check back on this every couple months as I think about writing a new SCP, and this is the first time I've noticed that the pizza box picture says 'Bon appetite' instead of the more correct spelling of 'bon appetit'.

Also that's an original picture. I had the pizza box, I wanted to write an SCP, so I got out a marker and wrote the message on the box and wrote the SCP around that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher Clarent's work obligations - as tested by Clarent by swinging at the air around her, saying "It was worth a try."

Laughed here. The good kind of laugh. Yes, the Foundation should be filled by professionals, but sometimes human nature gets the best of us. Little bit of humanization goes a long way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Using a cursed image to create a SCP
Loved it, absolute madman

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked the idea of this one and it was well executed.
The test logs were really interesting. It prompted some interesting questions.

+ show block
– hide block
  • Why are the researchers taking the note on the box as an authority? What happens if a subject is asked to take a spoon out of the box?
  • Can the effect be replicated with a mundane pizza box and marker?
  • If not, what happens if a subject takes SCP-2421-1 and places it in a replica of SCP-2421? Could a second subject enter the room and pick up SCP-2421-1 from either box? What if the first subject left beforehand?

It leaves me wanting more in a good way. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for an interesting item with some well-done testing logs, and what is some refreshingly good 'lolfoundation'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just lost it so hard at the eagle

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was surprised to poke in here after a while to 'check in' and see such a nice review! You seem to have gotten pretty much exactly what I was going for with the entry, right down to some guy having no idea what to do with an invisible knife and just writing a warning on the box he decided to store it in.

It was originally quite a bit more obvious, but the article benefited greatly from editing and suggestions and I think the implication is better than making it explicit.

As for comparison to other SCPs, well, I think I'm better than some, but I'm not going to compare this to anything from the first 200 or so! I just like the idea that while sometimes SCPs are scary or horrific, some are just "huh, what's up with that?" and are easy to contain, but still definitely need containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I just saw this response. First of all… sorry. Full disclosure: I was a little drunk when I posted that, and it probably came off more abrasive than I intended!

Secondly, you have a really fair point about different genres, styles and payoffs. What I take issue with is "different site eras," which I don't think is entirely fair. It's always rankled me that newer writers are held to a completely different standard than older writers ("older" here meaning both those who wrote pieces in 2008-2011 era, and also writers with a few SCPs under their belt). I understand the nostalgia factor, but what's the point of having a rating module at all on older SCPs if they're completely sacrosanct?

So as jerky-sounding as my last sentence is, I'm going to leave it. Just understand that by "087" I really mean "any Series I entry that would die instantly in a hailstorm of downvotes by today's standards." And by "you," I of course don't mean anyone in particular. Sort of the royal "you."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, you don't have to insult people to express your pleasure for an article, you know. I also believe that this and 087 are in different genres, written in different site eras, and have different writing styles and payoffs. Not really fair-game to compare the two.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Christ, people are tough to please these days!

This was delightful. The final log succeeded in its goal of ratcheting up the stakes. "Invisible knife" is a boring concept, but "invisible knife that exists only because you BELIEVE it does ya know probably" is a lot more intriguing. And finally, the thing I really loved about this article? The idea that you might not need the box at all.

Also I can't help but imagine that some random dude ordered a pizza from Joe's Non-Newtonian Pizza, got a box with an invisible knife, shrugged, cut up his pizza, ate it, and discarded the whole mess. Then he went to bed thinking, "Wow, invisible knives! What'll they think of next?"

Then he went to sleep and forgot about it. Months and years later, a team of scientists with six figure salaries are still puzzling over this schlub's trash.

This is delightful. If you downvoted or no-voted this but up voted 087 then you're either a hypocrite or just have exceptionally poor taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In retrospect, I'm reminded of an event back in high school.

We had a hypnotist come for an assembly. He hypnotized a group of students and told them to hold out their arms. Then, he said that a bird came and landed on their outstretched arm. Most of the students smiled and started to pet the little songbird they had. However, there was one guy who kept his arm away from his body and stared with much consternation at his bird.

The hypnotist approached him. "Is something wrong with your bird?"

The student replied, "It's an eagle."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of reminds me of the Ontological 6-Ball.

(And yeah, butter's fine.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're probably the first person to explicitly spell out what I was trying to get at with the SCP (I hope other people got it too!). But your interpretation of what happened during that test is just as valid as Decibelle's.

What if the box is totally non-anomalous in actual fact? What would happen if nobody wrote that warning on the box?
Did you ever really need a box?
Of course, that's one interpretation. Maybe an amateur pizza place is just accidentally leaving invisible knives in boxes.

Butter's chosen because it's relatively easy to cut through, keeps its shape, and goes well with knives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, the first thought is that D-788 simply pantomimed putting the knife in the box. It's an invisible knife.

Second, the test would involve being able to pull multiple knives from the box, which starts with the belief that there is a knife to be taken from the box. Thus, the test would involve being able to pull a knife from the box while believing that a knife was pulled from the box. Follow that up with a knife fight (is my knife tougher than your knife?) and the question becomes… does believing there is more than one knife in the box allow one to take multiple knives, or is it a question of understanding that the existence of a knife in a box does not require the existence of the box to ascertain the existence of the knife?

Also, 20 cm of butter? That's a lot of butter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and now I realize why the whole time I was reading this I was expecting AWCY

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did notice that they didn't have any associates. However, I still am convinced that the expungement is hiding the fact that they made them watch the Phantom Menace while eating pizza cut by the knife. Why no associates? Because Knight secretly enjoys the movie, and doesn't want anyone else to know.

D-788 sees Jar Jar Binks pop up on screen for the first time, and is filled with rage. "Fuck, I hate this. Who the fuck let Lucas think this was a good idea?" So he takes the invisible knife and stabs Jar Jar Binks, and he dies for the rest of the movie. This leads to them killing off more and more people in the movie until D-600 flips out.

"Enough with this shit!" After trying to wrestle the invisible knife away from D-788, security intervenes. Then later the next day, D-788 manages to get their own knife… somehow?

The placement of the expungement and the information we get before and after perfectly supports my line of thinking, because there's nothing to let on. I don't mind expungements. I don't hate them on principle, even, but they have to work well and be used well like any other tool. And while I enjoy literally everything else about this skip, I can't enjoy this bit there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take a look at the subjects line, as compared to the other two. There's a very slight difference in format. I'd hoped that that, plus the expungement, was enough to imply that while Senior Researcher Knight wasn't breaking any Foundation protocol or regulations, they didn't want other people to know what happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But then why have an expungement at all? Why not make something up? It just cheapens the article because then it becomes obvious that there's nothing really behind that expungement. It's just… something, and it's weird and doesn't help at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Correct - Clarent and one of the D-Class were given a pronoun for flow and clarity's sake, but it wasn't important to the others and I didn't mind people making up whatever in their head for what they look like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actual answer?
It didn't matter to me what they were instructed to do, beyond S.R. Knight trying to further push the boundaries of what the SCP could do. The only important thing is that it took long enough that one of the D-Class figured it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A pizza box with an invisible knife inside.

Okay then.

It's almost like a reimagining of 1057, where the shark is a knife and the pool is a pizza box. I dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They is a gender-neutral pronoun, so considering the consistency it's used, it looks intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "D-788 succeeded in escaping their cell and severely injuring" is this one person? It looks like a grammatical error. Your skip looks pretty solid, but I was disappointed and a little confused by the ending. Really close to an up-vote from me, but not quite yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel the same way about the final log, but enjoyed the rest enough to warrant an upvote. After seeing the pending class change at the start, I was eager to see what happened in the last log, but I ended up disappointed.
Really great article in pretty much every other way however

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So close to an upvote. So damn close. But…

That last test log tells me nothing. What you're saying is that the Foundation told D-Class that SCP-2124-1 would be tested against itself. Then in the next sentence, you say that they were instructed to… something. Didn't you just tell us that the Foundation told the D-Class they'd test the knife against itself? So why are they telling them something else? Plus, that expungement tells me nothing, except it results somehow in D-788 to be able to take SCP-2124-1 without needing the box.

So really, for all I know, they were instructed to watch The Phantom Menace and stab Jar Jar Binks whenever he was on screen, that obnoxious bastard, and the only thing I took away is that for… reasons, D-788 could be able to keep taking the knife.

Expungements have to hint at something. Even if they contradict previous information, they need to hint at something in a way that, despite my mind wandering about the possibilities, you can only go to one general, maybe even vague, conclusion. They can't be so vague and open-ended as for me to be able to arrive at fifty million other possibilities.

No-vote, because the rest of this is good and on-point with a mix of humor and seriousness that doesn't cheapen either one, but the last test log kills it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to neutral vote this *but* I'd say, for your first SCP, this is pretty good and good enough, I feel, to survive on the site. So far, looks to be doing that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the implications of that last test. +1

Edit: I read Deci's comment, and I agree fully. You need to hint at something with redactions. My vote is still +1 though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description says "CAREFUL! INVISIBLE KNIFE INSIDE BOX", but the image shows "CAREFUL! INVISIBLE KNIFE IN BOX". Just a small nitpick :) I'd change this. Otherwise, I'm not sure about this article, so no vote. It's definitely got the setup for something interesting, but I lose interest by the second test log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon staring at it for a while, I agree and have reordered the paragraphs and removed the cruft. Re-reading those paragraphs in that order makes it a lot more amusing and interesting.

Also took the opportunity to remove the phrase 'anomalously sharp' from the article. Cutting things like a normal sharp kitchen knife will do - it still cuts through things that a normal knife can't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hear ya there. Upvoting, mainly because of that line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I wish someone would make a knife sharp enough to cut through work obligations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The paragraphs are all in the wrong order. You don't get to describing the anomalous properties until you've seemingly done everything else there is to do in the Description.

Here's how I would order them:

Description: SCP-2124 is a large-sized pizza box,…
All components of SCP-2124, including the ink…
A human being attempting to consciously…
[the bullets]
SCP-2124-1 has no detectable weight or measurable presence, …
Exact dimensions and properties of instances of…
The interior of SCP-2124 appears to be empty…

And the two paragraphs I didn't mention aren't necessary at all.

I've also edited the first few paragraphs and testing logs to add paragraph spacing to them, since consecutive paragraphs should almost always have a blank space in between them if there's any possibility they could be confused for one paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2124 is the pizzabox - because there's not a good way to contain the knives, and they do seem to be connected somehow to the box.

The how-it-was found is meant to be convenient, and I thought about it for a while before deciding I couldn't think of a better way short of having a researcher find it themself. I wanted to imply that there could be hundreds of simple unknown things out there, just locked away in attics. But a better job could've been done, I'm sure.

As for Clarent's behaviour - I could have maybe done that better. I wanted to make it very clear that whatever properties SCP-2124-1 had, it definitely wasn't metaphysical beyond its… existence, for lack of a better word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mentioning the -1 early was intentional and I hoped it would become evident through context before it really got explained, for the same reason that I mention test 09 long before it pops up - because it's important to the article, and is worth getting to the end for.

There's a reason I call it 'the knife' during test logs, and that's because it's paraphrasing what the D-Class are told. They're not told to pick up SCP-2124-1, they're told to pick up a knife.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brief run-through:

+ show block
– hide block

consistent with an anomalously sharp kitchen knife

The question is: What constitutes an "anomalously sharp kitchen knife"? Your elaboration of it seems like a stronger version of a knife (that could cut through steel). It might do well to have a clearer (and empirically defined) description of the knife's sharpness.

On that note, what would an invisible kitchen knife need to be so sharp?

Researcher Clarent's work obligations - as tested by Clarent by swinging at the air around her, saying "It was worth a try."

Is this really necessary? It's borderline unprofessional behaviour and there is no indication that 2124-1 can "cut" through abstract concepts (That could be a cool concept to work with though).

Even so, how would cutting the air around oneself cut an abstract concept tied to oneself? It is slightly more logically to be cutting yourself or your brain or something… but I digress.

Pros:

  • Admittedly, this is the first time I see an imaginary knife drawn from a pizza box as though it were a sheath.

Cons:

  • The "how it was found" story felt way too convenient. Unless I am mistaken, a researcher's friend just messaged him that he found a potential SCP by chance. This implies unprofessionalism on part of the researcher (a major security compromise) and a level of coincidence that is jarring to believe in. A stronger origin may have been a random civilian accidentally using the SCP. While relatively mundane (and even cliche), it is easier to believe than a researcher's friend calling him about a possible anomalous item.
  • In the main bulk of the article, you never explicitly explained what SCP-2124 is exactly. I can infer that it is an invisible knife, but it took a while to realise that. It might do well to state SCP-2124-1's existence and what it is, before jumping to casually talking about it and its properties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is interesting, and isn't scary either. Just weird, which I like, as many new users try to make scary scp's and are usually unsuccessful.
Saying that, it seems to be lacking something, dunno what, so don't ask.
No vote for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had the same qualms as you but the last log saved it for me, and secured a +1. This article could be improved with a little trimming and another pass through for consistency, but I thought the idea was pretty good and the ending made it significantly better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You. You lurked moar.

It's always nice when someone's first submission shows that they took the time to understand what makes an article successful, and followed the guides properly. This was a simple concept, well executed. It's nothing super spectacular, but it kept me interested, and left me wanting a bit more, in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought that, while the idea was interesting, the execution needs work. You do stuff like mentioning the -1 before you explain what it is. Then in the test log, you just start calling it the knife. You really lost me at the second test, which is a big wall of text and made me lose interest in the article. There's a lot of potential here, but I don't think its been realized in this particular iteration.

item.title: A first attempt
item.content:encoded:
My first ever SCP, after being a reader for a long, long time.
Thanks to users sinchu9, minmin and RagnaOlir and a few others for their reviews of "It looks done", "nice and simple", "I like it, it's weird without trying to be creepy" and "it's different enough from the other pizza box".

Hopefully this won't be my last.

edit: username correction

edit2: If you see a grammar or stylistic issue, PLEASE edit it yourself, by all means! You have my blessing, go nuts!
